note: SNG-084 — every mechanic explains itself AT THE POINT IT BITES. Authored by Aevi 2026-07-12 (Design Law 15: copy is CONTENT, never hardcoded in app.js). `short` is the one-liner shown inline or on the ⓘ. `more` is the expandable why. **The REFUSALS matter most: a rule the player cannot distinguish from a bug IS a bug, and a silent 'no' is the worst thing in the game.**
voice: The game's voice: direct, a little dry, concrete. Explain the WHY, never just the what. Name the cost. Never apologise for a rule that is doing its job.

rules.progressive: Do not dump the system on a new player. Show `short` where the mechanic bites; `more` only on request.
rules.everyRefusalExplains: ⛔ NO LOCK MAY BE SILENT. Every barred thing says WHICH gate stopped it and why that gate exists.
rules.noJargonWithoutAGloss: If the copy says 'antipode' or 'ring distance', the ⓘ must define it right there.

entries.surface: a barred ability whose tradition is the player's antipode
entries.short: Closed — this is your antipode.
entries.more: You stand at one end of an axis; this craft belongs to the other. You chose an end, and choosing an end is what makes it mean something. **No amount of study opens this.** The only road to your own opposite is a BRAID — an ability that holds both poles at once — and nobody teaches those. You find them by doing something that should not have worked.

entries.surface: a capstone (tier IV–V) in an adjacent tradition
entries.short: Free to learn — but not their capstone. You are kin, not one of them.
entries.more: Your neighbours on the circle share your leanings, so their crafts come easily. But a people's deepest work is theirs. **Greatness is taught, not bought** — and they teach it to their own.

entries.surface: an ability several steps around the ring
entries.short: Costs extra — this craft is {steps} steps from anything you are.
entries.more: The further a craft sits from your dispositions, the more it costs you to hold. Not forbidden. Just expensive, and honestly so.

entries.surface: an ability above the secondary/tertiary tier cap
entries.short: Your secondary goes to tier III; your tertiary to tier II. This is deeper than that.
entries.more: You may dabble broadly or go deep narrowly. Depth in a craft you did not centre your life on is not on offer.

entries.surface: an ability of a people the player has no standing with
entries.short: You must be native to them, standing in their land, or taught by one of them.
entries.more: A people's craft is not a book you can read. You are born to it, you go to where it lives, or someone who holds it decides you are worth teaching. **Folk crafts are the exception** — the Valley's traditions are open to anyone, because the centre holds a little of everything.

entries.surface: an ability above the player's level
entries.short: You are not ready for this yet.
entries.more: Tiers are depth of mastery, not a shopping list. This one is further in than you have gone.

entries.surface: the breadth cap — 'N of N skills, at capacity'
entries.short: You can carry only so many crafts at once. Further points now DEEPEN what you know rather than add to it.
entries.more: **Breadth or depth. That is the trade, and it is meant to cost you.** A person who knows a little of everything is not the same as a person who knows one thing to its root — and the game will not let you pretend otherwise. If you want a different craft, you must give one up, or grow.

entries.surface: the great circle, wherever it is shown
entries.short: Twenty-four peoples on a ring. Twelve axes. Each people stands opposite the one it is the answer to.
entries.more: The world's physics is DISPOSITION. Every axis is a tension between two peoples — dark and light, death and life, order and chaos — and each people bears one end of it. **Your neighbours on the ring are your kin; the station directly across is your antipode, and it is closed to you.** This is not only a skill tree. It is the geography of the world, the map of who you can become, and the shape of what you gave up.

entries.surface: primary / secondary / tertiary
entries.short: Primary: everything. Adjacent: free, but not their capstones. Secondary: to tier III. Tertiary: to tier II.
entries.more: Where you stand determines what you can reach. **And what you choose closes something forever** — the far side of your own axis. That closure is the price of being someone in particular.

entries.surface: a cross-pole braid (known or rumoured)
entries.short: A braid holds an axis WHOLE — both poles at once. It is the only road to your own opposite.
entries.more: No people teaches these. You cannot buy one, and you cannot be trained in one. **You do something that should not have worked, and afterward you discover what you did.** To carry a braid is the rarest thing a character can do: to have refused to choose an end.

entries.surface: an action flagged NOVEL (−15)
entries.short: You are reaching past what you know. That is supposed to be dangerous.
entries.more: A novel act carries a −15 surcharge, a wider chance of a bad botch, and backlash if it fails. **The game does not punish you for improvising — it charges you for it, and it pays you back if you survive.**

entries.surface: a known discovery (+20)
entries.short: You did this, you survived it, and you repeated it until it was yours. The same act that cost you −15 now pays +20.
entries.more: That is a thirty-five point swing, and it is the heart of how this game grows you. **Power is not bought. It is earned by reaching past yourself and living.**

entries.surface: after a roll where the place helped or hindered
entries.short: The place favoured this. / The place was against you.
entries.more: **Geography is disposition.** A place leans one way; when you act with its grain it lifts you, and when you act against it, it drags. Some places lean so hard that a stranger can barely function there — and your own antipode's country is the hardest ground in the world for you specifically.

entries.surface: the difficulty read on an action
entries.short: Routine · Hard · Very hard. The GM sets it from the fiction, not from your sheet.
entries.more: The world does not scale itself to you. Some things are simply beyond you right now, and the game will say so rather than quietly making them easy.

entries.surface: acting at 0 energy
entries.short: Empty. Everything is −10 until you rest — but you can still act.
entries.more: Exhaustion is a cost, not a wall. You may always try. You will simply be worse at it, and you will know why.

entries.surface: the energy bar, and the first time it runs low
entries.short: Energy does not come back on its own. You recover by eating, resting, sleeping, or sitting still on purpose.
entries.more: **There is no passive regeneration.** A breather buys you a little; a night's sleep buys you most of it; meditation scales with what you have cultivated. **Rest is an ACT in this game, and forgetting to make time for it is a real mistake with real consequences.**

entries.surface: the rank pips on an ability
entries.short: Rank deepens ONE craft. Breadth adds another. You cannot do both at once.
entries.more: Ranks I–III take an ability from a thing you can do to a thing you ARE. At rank III some abilities FORK — and **the path you do not take closes forever.**

entries.surface: tier I–V on an ability
entries.short: Depth of mastery within a people's craft. I–III you can be taught. IV–V is a capstone — those are given, not sold.
entries.more: A capstone is one profound thing, not a longer version of a small thing. It is why you must have standing with a people to hold theirs.

entries.surface: the `notFor` line on any ability
entries.short: Every craft has a thing it cannot do. This is that thing.
entries.more: **Nothing in this world is a universal tool.** The limits are not balance patches — they are what the craft IS. A ward that could also cut would not be a ward.

entries.surface: the function chips (heal · shield · strike · reveal · conceal · bind · move · break · ward)
entries.short: What this craft actually DOES, regardless of what it is called.
entries.more: Two peoples on opposite sides of the world may both `reveal` — one by burning the dark away, one by learning to see in it. **The function is the same. What it costs you is not.**

entries.surface: the moment a combination is minted
entries.short: You did two things together, enough times, and they became one thing. It has a name now.
entries.more: The engine noticed. It always was noticing. **You did not buy this and nobody taught it to you** — you found it by doing it, and it is yours in a way a purchased thing never is.

entries.surface: the combination list
entries.short: Within-tradition · kin-civilization · cross-axis · cross-pole. In that order of difficulty.
entries.more: What you can braid tracks what you are NEAR. Your own people's crafts braid easily; your neighbours' with effort; the far side of the world only by accident and grace. **And the braid across your own axis — cross-pole — is the hardest thing there is.**

entries.surface: the Plan a Gambit affordance
entries.short: Connected challenges, each solvable several ways, planned ACROSS before you run them.
entries.more: A gambit is not a longer turn. It is a different KIND of turn: you see the whole shape, and you commit to it. **The elements make a gambit — not the number of steps.**

entries.surface: the step count while building a gambit
entries.short: Fewer steps is HARDER. You have less slack.
entries.more: **Counter-intuitive and load-bearing:** every step is somewhere the plan can absorb a failure. A tight plan is elegant and brittle. A generous one survives a bad roll — because **invoking a fallback ADDS a step, and the gambit GROWS as it goes wrong.** A single-path plan dies on one bad number. A gambit does not.

entries.surface: the running energy total in the gambit builder
entries.short: Each step costs. The total is what you are actually spending.
entries.more: You can plan something you cannot afford. The builder will let you. It will also tell you.

entries.surface: the xp bar
entries.short: You earn xp by DOING — every rolled action pays, and failure pays too.
entries.more: Crit 8 · success 5 · partial 3 · **failure 2** · a botch still pays 2. **Reaching and missing is worth more than not reaching.** Novel acts pay a bonus on top. The game rewards the attempt.

entries.surface: levelling up
entries.short: A level buys: +1 attunement · +5 reserves · a sub-attribute point · a skill point · one more craft you may carry.
entries.more: Levels widen what you can hold. They do not, by themselves, make you good at anything — that is what you spend them on.

entries.surface: the attribute dots + the soft cap at 4
entries.short: The first four points in an attribute are worth far more than the fifth.
entries.more: Competence is cheap; mastery is expensive. Going from hopeless to solid is the easy part of any life.

entries.surface: when an aptitude is earned
entries.short: The game has been watching HOW you play, not just what you picked.
entries.more: Play things carefully and you become a strategist. Talk your way through and you become silver-tongued. **These accrue to the CHARACTER, not to you** — Cellaceron can be a strategist while Usnea is not, even though you play both.

entries.surface: the danger marker on a location
entries.short: How dangerous this place is to ANYONE. How dangerous it is to YOU also depends on how far it sits from what you are.
entries.more: A pure pole — the Blaze, the Unlit Deep, the Grand Lattice — is brutal to everyone, and lethal to someone from its opposite. **You do not unlock a region. You become able to survive it.**

entries.surface: a folk tradition (Valley Craft · Harmonic · Radiant)
entries.short: Open to anyone. No standing required.
entries.more: These are the crafts of the near-centre, where all the axes cross faintly. **The centre can hold a little of everything — which is exactly why anyone may learn from it.**

entries.surface: a precursor ability
entries.short: Not a people's craft. The substrate the world is built on.
entries.more: You do not learn this by studying. Something in the old lattice has to touch you first — and it does not ask.

entries.surface: a dimmed entity on the map or in the codex
entries.short: You have heard of this. You have not seen it.
entries.more: Rumour is real information and the game treats it as such — but it is not knowledge, and it is drawn faintly to remind you of the difference.

entries.surface: the STAKES line on any quest
entries.short: This is what happens if you walk away.
entries.more: **Every quest names the cost of ignoring it, because walking away is a real choice with a real price — not a failure state.** The world will proceed without you, and it will find you later.

entries.surface: the route list on a quest
entries.short: There is more than one way through this. Your domains change which ways are open.
entries.more: **A challenge with a single solution is a lock, not a story.** Force, guile, care, reason, craft, concealment, truth, endurance — the game does not have a preferred answer, and it will not punish you for solving things your own way.

entries.surface: the bond number on a companion
entries.short: They are deciding about you, the same way you are deciding about them.
entries.more: Bond grows by what you do near them, not by what you say. At depth, they teach you something no tradition will.

entries.surface: the content-rating setting
entries.short: This sets how intense the narration and the generated world get, for THIS person.
entries.more: G through R+. **Two floors are always on regardless of the ceiling:** never any prohibited content, and a minor is never portrayed in romantic or sexual terms. Those do not move, for anyone, ever. **In a shared world each player sees it through their own ceiling — so a family can play one world at different intensities without anyone bleeding into anyone else's.**
